sentences.text: OK.
As we were shipped out from Stutthof, as we were going one way, there was another transport coming in.
And in that transport, I saw my cousins, who also were brought in.
Because they had been in Estonia, and they were-- they were brought back to Stutthof.
As we were going out, they were coming in.
And all we could do is just wave.
And we went one way, and they went the other way.
So we knew at that time that they're still alive.
And that was my cousin and her sister.
Like the two cousins.
Well, one is Lily.
And the other is Souri.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's as much as we knew about them then.
And then we went on, and they took us in trucks until we got to Torun.
It's in Poland, but that was occupied by the Germans.
I mean, that part of Poland-- I imagine all of Poland was occupied by Germans, by Germany.
And the town was called Torun.
And from Torun, we were taken to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a small place.
Like, it wasn't really a town for working camp.
And what we were doing, we were digging ditches behind the front lines for the soldiers, you know, as they stand in ditches.
And, you know, we were always doing them further and further.
And at night, we would be taken back to camp.
And the camp consisted of tents, again.
Big, round tents with-- and it was winter in Poland.
It was very cold.
And by that time, our hair started to grow a little.
Our hair was about an inch, an inch and a half long by then.
And as we were sleeping in the tents, our hair would even sort of freeze to the tent, you know, because we were so close.
You know?
And all around.
In the middle was the stove.
So in the morning when we would get up, we would stand around the little stove to get dressed.
And what were you wearing?
Well, by that time, see, until our shoes, our own shoes lasted, we wore them.
And after that, we were given whatever.
But when we were in Torun already, we were given the wooden shoes and dresses which used to be what they collected in Auschwitz or whatever, because we didn't have a uniform.
We were wearing those dresses.
Well, some had a coat.
Some had rags.
We used to put rags around our feet and our legs.
And those wooden shoes were big, so we were able to put rags.
And that's what we were wearing.
And the 15-- out of the 1,500 of us, slowly, about 700 or 800 died.
I mean naturally, without being shot or killed, but they were so weak already that they died.
Actually, I think it was by trucks, as far as I could remember.
In trucks.
It wasn't a train.
And we-- there, we had the SS soldiers guarding us.
And the-- we had-- like, the camp had one officer who was called the oberscharfuhrer, who was, like, over us.
Like, if the SS wanted something, they would have to communicate with the oberscharfuhrer to get us to do this or that or the other.
But they would be watching us so we don't want to escape.
Or we-- certain things that nobody communicates with us.
Because there was a mother and daughter who-- she befriended one of the SS.
And he was sort of like a friend to her.
He would bring her food and bring her things.
But then that was stopped.
And then her mother died, and she was left by herself.
And we would go out to work every morning and come back at night to-- and we were given our rations.
And like I-- one slice-- one bread, size of a brick, and divided into five.
And black coffee in the morning, before we went out to work.
And a plate of soup at night, a thick plate of soup.
I don't know what was in it.

But because this lady, this woman befriended my sister so much, and she was sort of managing the kitchen, she used to tell her, after everyone is served, you come back with your dish, and we'll give you an extra plate of soup.
And which later, she would hang around, come back to the-- to the tent.
We would eat fast, our soup, and she'd go back.
And most of the time, she would get an extra, which was a big help.
And that was because that lady we befriended in Stutthof.
No, unfortunately I don't.
I don't.
But I could see her.
I could see her before my eyes.
And that was until we were with some people whose father was a rabbi, some women.
And they sort of kept track what day it is, what month it is, which we didn't remember.
They were a little older than us.
And I remember it was Yom Kippur.
And we went out to-- we had to go out to work.
I mean, it wasn't cold, so cold yet.
And we were out at work, and we were working all day, and we were fasting.
I mean, some of us.
I'm not saying that the whole camp did.
But some of us were fasting and working and came back.
And it wasn't nighttime yet.
It was like-- so we were-- we went to get our ration and our soup.
And we were sitting in the-- outside the tent and waiting until we could see stars so we could eat.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we did.
And then we used to see planes flying overhead, you know, when we were working.
And we would always wonder, are these the American planes?
Are they Russian?
Are they German?
You know, we were always hoping that soon, soon, soon, we'll be freed, and this is what we were talking about all the time.
I'd like to go back to Yom Kippur.
Do you remember what else you did besides fast?
Did anybody pray?
No.
No prayer books and no praying.
Just what we knew by heart.
But the lady who we befriended who was managing the kitchen, like, when we friended-- like, she befriended us in-- in Stutthof.
When she came over, like, we were sitting-- like I said, we weren't existing, as far as the camp was concerned, like, because they thought that they-- they finished with us.
But we went into this bunk, and like to this place.
And she was also in that place.
And before she came over to us, she heard us pray, my sister and I.
You know, every Saturday night before it's out, before Shabbos is out, we always say [HEBREW].
You know?
Like, because I remember with my mother, [CRIES] every Saturday night, we used to sit.
My father was in shul, in the synagogue, and she would sit with us.
And all together, we would say [HEBREW].
And we continued that in camp.
And when she heard us, she came over, and that's when she told us that she would try and get us into this transport to go when she is going.
And that's the only prayers, really.
No.
No praying at all.
There was no-- there was no time and.
There was no-- no prayer books or anything like that.
No.
And of course, we didn't know anything by heart.
I mean, in the morning, you know, sometimes we'd say [HEBREW] in the morning when we'd get up.
And sometimes we wouldn't even say that.
But that stays in my mind vividly, on Yom Kippur, how we waited until we could see the stars and eat.
And every day, we-- my sister and I-- we-- we said that every day, we'll save a slice of bread.
You know?
After that, because we got the two portions, the two of us.
So one slice we would save, and that would dry up, you know, and we'd keep it in a-- in a bag.
It was always kept under our heads, you know?
Because everybody was stealing whatever they could.
I mean, that's the way it was, everybody for themselves.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And that bread really helped us.
And I remember one night, one of the girls who I slept beside was very ill.
And she didn't want to go to the infirmary, like everybody was scared to go to the infirmary.
This was already in-- you know, it wasn't the-- the concentration camp, like the work [INAUDIBLE].
And she was so sick, and she was shaking and trembling and yelling.
Then some of the girls picked her up and put her in the center of the tent.
You know?
And then the people were walking around, just not far from the little stove what we had.
By morning, she was dead.
Poor girl.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know what they did with the dead from there, because we never questioned it.
And we used to have to go outside to wash.
You know?
There was a pump outside, in the middle of the camp, in the morning, where we would wash our-- like, you know, wash.
There was no bath.
We were full of lice.
Full of lice.
And like-- not head lice.
Well, head lice, too.
But it was in the clothing.
And we were more so there than in any other of the camps, because we had to wear so much clothing by then because it started to get cold.
And we would-- they would-- they would take us for disinfecting, you know, once.
And every few weeks, we would go and have our clothes and ourselves-- and ourselves disinfected.
Didn't help, because they would hatch again.
I mean, they just couldn't kill them all.
So sometimes our pastime was taking off our clothes and picking the lice.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was going on like that.
You know?
Different work, but mostly we would make the ditches.
And we were given a certain amount that we had to do a day.
And we were, you know, I don't know, sometimes 10 of us were given a certain amount.
And of course, my sister was always where I was.
She just didn't-- as far as I knew then, she didn't want to work.
She would stand on her shovel, you know, like lean on her shovel, and didn't want to do anything.
And I would be afraid that once the SS noticed her, they would take her out, and God knows what's going to happen to her.
And I would beg her, Ella, please, please.
I can't.
I can't, and I can't.
And she just wouldn't do it.
And we had to do her share too.
Now she tells me, I didn't have the strength.
I couldn't.
It's not that I didn't want.
I just couldn't do the work.
But nothing happened, and she made it.
And that was going on for months in that camp, day after day, the same thing.
You know, some would die.
Some with get sick, that they couldn't hide it anymore, and they would go to the infirmity.
And sometimes they would come back from the infirmary, because it was not a concentration camp, so they weren't taken to the crematoriums or anything, because there was none there.
But most of them died.
And out of the 1,500, about 700 or 800 died.
We must have been about 50 in each tent.
They were big tents.
And we were sleeping a round-- like it was round tents.
And there was straw on there, and a blanket over the straw.
Like, we each would have our spare place and a blanket to cover.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was our sleeping accommodations.
In the center, yeah.
In the middle, there was a stove.
And we would collect wood from where we work.
And we always would bring it back and make a fire.
Nothing else.

In the center was the stove, which was with a little bit-- and around it, there was sort of a little-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, it was something like an edge that we-- you know, we wouldn't walk.
And then there was room to walk around.
You know.
As we came in the door, we would go onto that walk and then climb over.
Which wasn't much of a climbing, but you know, just something to keep the straw from the center.
We would just go onto our beds.
And we would sit there and-- and lice out.
And clean the lice.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after a while, you know, less and less people, we were less and less.
And we would already hear the shooting, you know, like from far, like, far from distance.
But we knew there was something going on.
And then one day, we got up, and we were told that they're moving out.
And that was in January, 1945.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That we were moving out of this camp.
And the oberscharfuhrer, who was our [INAUDIBLE] officer said, that they will have-- we will be marching, and we will be sort of about five or so in a line and marching.
And the sick, he said, will go with us, but they will be taken on a-- in a cart, which it was so, yes.
They were put on a cart and taken.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But two of the-- there were three sisters together, and one of them was sick.
And she was in the infirmary.
And they didn't believe that the sick ones will come with us.
So the two sisters asked to get her sister out from the infirmary, and she will be able to walk.
Of course, she didn't.
She was very weak.
And the two sisters, like, they would hold her, one on one side, one on the other side.
And they walked for a while, but nobody wanted to be the last or close to the last.
Everybody wanted to try to be at least in the middle or in the front of the procession, and-- because the SS soldiers were walking with us on both sides and in the back.
And if they saw anybody who wasn't able to, they would just take them out to the side of the road and shoot them and leave them.

And I'll never forget, when these three sisters were pulling their sister-- the two were pulling their sisters, and the SS noticed, they just took her out from their arms to the side of the road and shot her right in front of them, in front of everybody.
And we had to go on.
And we were walking.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at one place, we came to a bridge.
And we were asked to go fast, fast, fast across the bridge.
Not to linger on, you know, go real fast.
And as soon as we crossed the bridge, the bridge was blown up, because I guess they thought that that will keep the Russians from advancing so fast.
And we kept on going for about five days.
And that's when our saved bread came to very good use, because some days we didn't get anything to eat at all.
And we had about 50 slices of bread saved, my sister and I.
So we would eat that, and we would share it with the others who didn't have anything.
Because a lot of them just-- when they got their rations, they would eat it up, and that was it.
It was very-- came in really handy, that saving.
Sometimes when we stopped at night, they would give us something.
But most of the time, no.
No.
They were just rushing us through.
And then as we were-- as we were-- about the fifth day, as we were walking, the oberscharfuhrer stopped us.

And he said that the SS soldiers, apparently, the way he put it, were going to shoot us all, because they had to leave, because the Russian army was approaching very fast, and they wouldn't want to fall prisoners.
And they had to leave.
And they didn't want to leave us, so they were going to shoot us.
But the oberscharfuhrer said, I will take care of them.
And I remember him putting on-- you know, with bullets, a whole-- and-- because otherwise, he wasn't carrying any guns, you know?
And he put that on, and he stopped us, and he was telling us, look, the SS soldiers are leaving.
You're in my care now, and I will be taking care of you.
And because that's what he said.
The Russian army is approaching, and we-- they-- they have to go.
They don't want to become prisoners.
And they were going to shoot-- he-- he said it right then.
They were going to shoot us.
But he said that he's going to take over, and we should behave.
And did we have any choice?
What were you thinking?
Like I said, we weren't thinking.
I'll tell you, because really, all the time I was in camp, from the first day on, we never menstruated.
There must have been something put in our food that not to think and not to have anything.
Something like that.
So all this time, I imagine-- because really, we couldn't think straight.
We were just-- one thing we were thinking, there's going to come a day soon that we'll be freed.
We have to hang on.
We have to hang on, and we'll go home, and we'll find our parents, and everything will be back as before.
So naive, at least we were.
Very naive.
So you thought everybody was still alive.
Yes.
Yes.
Although in Auschwitz, those fires, which I knew now, but I didn't know then, that the crematoriums were so overloaded.
That they were burning people, and they made a big ditch, and that's what the big fires were.
But I didn't know what those big fires were when we arrived.
That's what kept us going, yes.
That's what kept us going.
And as we were working with the oberscharfuhrer, like whatever was left of us, we came to this big estate.
It was like a big farm.
Like a very nice house, like more of a castle, like.
And a lot of stables, and a lot of cows and all that.
And we were put there for the night.
Well, we had passed [INAUDIBLE], which is-- was in German called Bromberg.
That was about-- must have been about 20 miles past that.
But I don't know the name of the town where that estate was.
But we stayed there overnight.
When we arrived, we were given some food, and we stayed overnight.
And I still remember that within the courtyard, the SS were gone.
It was just oberscharfuhrer.
And we were trying to milk some of the cows to get some milk.
Well, we never had milk all this time.
And later on-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] actually, the next day.
We slept over.
The next day, we were running around catching the cows to try and milk them, because we were sleeping in the stables with the cows and with the, you know, pigs and horses.
Whatever there was, you know? and the officer and the ones-- like there was the-- this woman who I said that she was the manager.
Like, they were sleeping in the main house, you know?
And the higher ranking were watching us.
But we were in the stables.
And how many people were left at this point?
At this point, we were about 700 of us, at this point.
Well, there were many stables.
We were all around.
There was all of us, except the higher ranking ones were sleeping in the house.
And the next morning, after we tried to milk these cows, and we got something to eat, he gathered us all up, and he was holding an-- a zelapel, like, you know, counting us.
And he was saying, look.
He says, as you see, the SS have left, and I am watching you, but I have to leave, because my-- I want to go back to my family.
I don't want to be-- become a prisoner of-- the Russians are approaching, and approaching very fast.
And the German army is retreating, and they are going to go-- because this is a main highway, they are going to go through here.
So my advice to you is to go off on the fields, not on the main highways, because you'll find German soldiers, and you're going to be either shot, or caught, or taken on with them.
So try and get on the fields.
So it was about seven of us who were friends at that time, my sister, and there were five-- there-- three of them were sisters, and two others, we decided we'd leave.
It was already getting a little bit dark at night that we leave.
The snow was deep.
It was January.
And we went on the fields.
You know?
We left there, and we took off on the fields.
And far away, we saw a little light, like a little house and a light.
So we were aiming for that.
And as we were coming closer and closer, we saw it was a farmhouse.
We came up to the door, and we knocked at the door.
And this man-- we couldn't talk to him.
He was a Polish man.
Because by that time, the Germans had run off whoever was settled there.
It was a Polish farm.
And as he opened the door, we saw some of the people from our-- were already sitting there in the kitchen by them.
And we said that we are seven of us, and we would like him to give us some place.
He says, look, I can't let you stay in the house.
I'm full.
But if you want, you could stay in the stable.
And we said fine.
And he took us into the stable, and there was seven of us.
It was nice and warm there with the animals.
The next day, he brought us some food, some soup.
And we had that once a day.
The day after, he brought us some soup.
And we sat there in the stable, and we just heard all the shooting and the things going on outside.
But far away, because this wasn't on the road, this-- this house.
And after three days, he came in.
And he said to us, the Russians are here.
They occupied this area.
The Germans are gone.
I have no more food.
I would like you to leave.
And he said-- well, he spoken in a broken German, which we understood.
He said-- so we asked, how-- where-- how are we going to go.
And he said, go-- showed us the way to go back from where we came from, to this estate.
And we set out, and we went.
And in the middle, the-- we met Russian soldiers, and they started yelling, [RUSSIAN], you know, which is stop.
Although they saw us with our wooden shoes, with the clothes, with the Jewish-- with the red stars on them.
Whether they didn't know or didn't want to know that we are-- or they thought maybe we are just disguised.
And they stopped us.
And they took us back to be the same estate where we had left from.
And there was the Russian headquarters set up.
And they brought us to their officer.
And he said, what do you bring these for to me?
He was yelling to them.
And they were drinking.
They were screaming.
They were yelling, the soldiers.
There was a German dead soldier lying on the floor.
And they were dancing around him, drinking around him, yelling.
And they gave us some bread.
By that time, I mean, the second officer told them, why did you bring these?
These are from the concentration camp.
They gave us bread.
But their bread was so bad, that even after the German bread what we ate, we couldn't eat it.
And-- I'm going to stop there, and we'll change the tape.
